data_source_patterns.overview: This file provides comprehensive patterns and examples to help identify whether a data source should use REST or JDBC connector approach.

category.name: web_services
category.description: Modern web services and SaaS applications

example.name: GitHub API
example.indicators: - REST API with endpoints like /repos, /users, /issues - JSON responses - OAuth2 or personal access token authentication - Rate limiting via headers

example.name: Salesforce REST API
example.indicators: - REST API endpoints for objects (Account, Contact, etc.) - OAuth2 authentication - JSON responses with nested objects - Pagination via nextRecordsUrl
example.routing_decision: REST connector (but note: Salesforce also has JDBC driver)

example.name: ServiceNow
example.indicators: - Table API with REST endpoints - Basic auth or OAuth2 - JSON responses - Query parameters for filtering

example.name: Jira Cloud API
example.indicators: - REST API v3 endpoints - API token authentication - JSON responses - Pagination with startAt/maxResults

example.name: AWS Services (S3, DynamoDB, etc.)
example.indicators: - REST/HTTP APIs - AWS signature authentication - JSON/XML responses - Service-specific endpoints

example.name: Google Cloud APIs
example.indicators: - REST APIs with JSON - OAuth2 authentication - Standard Google API patterns

example.name: Azure REST APIs
example.indicators: - REST endpoints - Azure AD authentication - JSON responses

indicators.indicator: API key, OAuth2, or basic authentication
Pagination via query parameters or headers

example.name: PostgreSQL
example.indicators: - Relational database - JDBC driver: org.postgresql.Driver - Connection string: jdbc:postgresql://host:port/database - SQL query support

example.name: MySQL / MariaDB
example.indicators: - Relational database - JDBC driver: com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver - Connection string: jdbc:mysql://host:port/database - SQL query support

example.name: Oracle Database
example.indicators: - Enterprise relational database - JDBC driver: oracle.jdbc.OracleDriver - Connection string: jdbc:oracle:thin:@host:port:sid - SQL and PL/SQL support

example.name: Microsoft SQL Server
example.indicators: - Relational database - JDBC driver: com.microsoft.sqlserver.jdbc.SQLServerDriver - Connection string: jdbc:sqlserver://host:port;database=dbname - T-SQL support

example.name: IBM Db2
example.indicators: - Enterprise relational database - JDBC driver: com.ibm.db2.jcc.DB2Driver - Connection string: jdbc:db2://host:port/database - SQL support

category.name: data_warehouses
category.description: Data warehouse systems (may support both REST and JDBC)

example.name: Amazon Redshift
example.indicators: - PostgreSQL-compatible data warehouse - JDBC driver available - SQL query support - Also has REST API via AWS SDK
example.routing_decision: Typically JDBC connector (but can use REST for AWS API)

example.name: Teradata
example.indicators: - Enterprise data warehouse - JDBC driver: com.teradata.jdbc.TeraDriver - SQL support

category.name: nosql_with_jdbc
category.description: NoSQL databases that provide JDBC drivers

example.name: Apache Cassandra (via JDBC)
example.indicators: - NoSQL database with JDBC driver - CQL (Cassandra Query Language) - JDBC driver available

example.name: MongoDB (via JDBC)
example.indicators: - Document database - JDBC driver available (MongoDB Connector for BI) - SQL-like query interface

hybrid_patterns.description: Data sources that support both REST API and JDBC connectivity. For these, ask the user which approach they prefer.

example.name: Snowflake
example.rest_support: - SQL REST API - OAuth2 authentication - JSON responses
example.jdbc_support: - JDBC driver: net.snowflake.client.jdbc.SnowflakeDriver - Connection string: jdbc:snowflake://account.snowflakecomputing.com - Full SQL support
example.recommendation: JDBC is more common for Snowflake, but REST API is good for specific use cases or when JDBC driver cannot be used.

example.name: Google BigQuery
example.rest_support: - BigQuery REST API - OAuth2 authentication - JSON responses - Query jobs via API
example.jdbc_support: - JDBC driver: com.google.cloud.bigquery.jdbc.Driver - Connection string: jdbc:bigquery://project-id - SQL support
example.recommendation: Both approaches are valid. JDBC for traditional SQL workflows, REST API for more programmatic control.

example.name: Salesforce
example.rest_support: - Salesforce REST API - OAuth2 authentication - SOQL queries via API - JSON responses
example.jdbc_support: - JDBC driver available (CData or similar) - SOQL support - Connection via JDBC
example.recommendation: REST API is more native to Salesforce. JDBC drivers are third-party and may have limitations.

example.name: Databricks
example.rest_support: - Databricks REST API - Token authentication - JSON responses
example.jdbc_support: - JDBC driver: com.databricks.client.jdbc.Driver - Connection to SQL endpoints - Spark SQL support
example.recommendation: JDBC is standard for SQL queries. REST API for cluster management and other operations.

when_to_prefer_rest.reason: User needs to access specific API endpoints
JDBC driver is not available or difficult to obtain
Need to access non-tabular data or metadata
API provides better rate limiting or quota management

when_to_prefer_jdbc.reason: User needs full SQL query capabilities
Need to perform complex joins or aggregations
Better performance for large data transfers
Standard database operations (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE)

keyword_indicators.usage_note: Use these keywords as indicators, but always confirm with the user. Multiple high-weight keywords strongly suggest a particular type.
